html:
CLMHF / Calgro M3: The Ugly Version Is the Opportunity

Calgro M3 is mispriced, but not for the reason the older drafts suggest. This is not a net-cash hidden-annuity story. It is a levered South African affordable-housing developer with a growing, high-margin memorial parks business, trading at roughly 0.27x NAV and about 2.6x forward FY26 earnings while moving into a much larger, longer-duration asset base.

The correction matters because the market’s error is still real. At the 15 May 2026 JSE close of R4.28, Calgro’s primary listing implies about US$0.26 using USD/ZAR of roughly 16.69. The OTC line still shows CLMHF at $0.28, but OTC liquidity is stale and thin. The real market is the JSE line. Against the company’s latest reported NAV of R15.82 per share, the equity trades at only 27% of book.

The market is still treating Calgro like a fragile construction contractor. The forward reality is closer to a discounted land-bank monetization vehicle with operating earnings, a 15-year pipeline, a credible debt-reduction path, and a death-care segment that now behaves like a cash engine rather than a science project.

The key transition is Bankenveld District City. Calgro and Eris acquired a 60/40 joint venture interest in what management describes as the last large-scale undeveloped property in the Sandton area, close to Sandton, Waterfall City, Alexandra, and the Marlboro Gautrain station. FY25 disclosure says the secured pipeline was R29.2 billion with 36,000 opportunities, including at least 20,000 units from Bankenveld. The 1H FY26 results show the cost of this transition clearly: revenue and HEPS fell because capital was redirected into Bankenveld infrastructure before revenue can be recognized from public-sector agreements.

That short-term earnings drag is the setup. Calgro is spending R158 million across FY26 and FY27 on phase-one Bankenveld road infrastructure, while legacy projects already have 2,589 serviced opportunities available and 871 units under construction. Residential revenue fell 11.6% in 1H FY26, but unit sales more than doubled to R317.5 million, gross margin held at 27.4%, and total group gross margin stayed at 29.4%, above the company’s 20%-25% target range. That is not distress behavior.

The memorial parks business is the second leg, and it is better to underwrite it cleanly than repeat the prior “perpetual trust annuity” claim. The official accounting review says maintenance is an IAS 37 liability, not deferred maintenance revenue. So the right thesis is not “free trust-fee annuity.” The right thesis is simpler and still attractive: Calgro owns and operates six private memorial parks, sells burial rights for cash, uses lay-by plans to build a visible order book, and earns roughly 50% gross margins.

In FY25, memorial parks revenue rose to 8% of group revenue from 4%, cash receipts grew 40%, lay-by receipts rose 57.5% to R24.6 million, and the active lay-by book was about R42 million with a 9% cancellation/default rate, per the FY25 SENS announcement. In 1H FY26, memorial parks revenue rose again to R39.8 million, gross profit was R20.1 million, and cash collections exceeded R50 million. Platinum City Memorial Park is expected to add more than 28,000 burial opportunities, with burials expected to start in the last quarter of FY26.

The social and retail context is almost nonexistent, which is part of the edge. OTC feeds show almost no volume, TipRanks/retail coverage is negligible, and the only real retail mention I found was a small Reddit value-investing thread calling out the low P/E and obscurity. This is not crowded. The shareholder base is not underwriting Bankenveld, memorial parks, or the NAV discount with any sophistication.

The balance sheet is the biggest objection. Net debt was R1.06 billion at August 2025, net debt/equity was 0.71, and operating cash flow was negative in 1H FY26 because of working capital and infrastructure spend. That is real. But the debt is not currently breaking the thesis: DSCR improved to 1.58 versus a 1.2 covenant minimum, management says covenants remain healthy, and sponsored Chronux research estimates R595 million of non-core asset sales plus R120 million of commercial/retail land sales could be used to reduce debt. If even a meaningful fraction of that happens, the equity impact is large because the market cap is only about R400 million to R450 million.

Valuation is the entire point. Chronux’s 1H FY26 report, which is sponsored research but still useful for triangulation, estimates FY26 HEPS of 167c, FY27 HEPS of 204c, and FY28 HEPS of 240c. At R4.28, that is 2.6x FY26 earnings, 2.1x FY27 earnings, and 1.8x FY28 earnings. The same report values the stock at R9.00 using a conservative SOTP: residential NAV at a 10% discount, memorial parks at only 4x EBITDA, other investments at book, and net debt deducted.

That R9.00 base case is not heroic. It is still only about 57% of current reported NAV and less than 5x FY27 earnings. A bull case does not require fantasy multiples. If Bankenveld moves from infrastructure spend to top-structure sales, non-core assets reduce debt, and memorial parks keeps compounding cash collections, 0.75x forward NAV or 6x FY28 earnings supports a mid-teens rand stock.

Macro has turned from a headwind into a delayed tailwind. SARB held the repo rate at 6.75% in March 2026, with cuts delayed by inflation and geopolitical risk, but housing data is already recovering. Ooba reported Q1 2026 home-loan application volumes up 15.9% from the Q4 2023 trough, granted bond value up 36.6%, first-time buyers at 48% of applications, and approval rates at 84%. BetterBond also reported Q1 2026 applications up 9.7% quarter over quarter. Calgro’s affordable and gap-housing exposure sits directly in the part of the market that benefits when banks loosen and rates eventually fall.

The downside case is debt and timing. If Bankenveld public-sector agreements stall, asset sales disappoint, rates stay high, and working capital remains a cash drain, the stock can trade like a levered micro-cap developer rather than a NAV discount story. Rand weakness also matters for a US OTC holder. But the downside is partially cushioned by real serviced inventory, a high-margin memorial parks cash stream, reported NAV far above the share price, active share buybacks, and a dividend policy that has already returned cash when balance sheet conditions allowed.

The variant perception is that Calgro is not a broken developer with a cute side business. It is a cheap equity stub on a real asset base, currently absorbing transition costs before Bankenveld and memorial parks become more visible. The market is pricing the accounting mess, South Africa discount, OTC neglect, and debt burden. It is not pricing 15 years of pipeline, 50% memorial parks gross margins, a forward P/E below 3x, or the possibility that asset sales can delever the company by more than the current market cap.

Current price: CLMHF last OTC print $0.28; JSE primary close R4.28, about $0.26 look-through.

Bear target: R2.50 / $0.15, down 46% from $0.28. This assumes no re-rating, slow sales, pressure on covenants, and the stock falling to about 0.16x NAV.

Base target: R9.00 / $0.54, up 93% from $0.28. This assumes FY26/FY27 earnings roughly hold, memorial parks continues growing, and the market gives Calgro only 0.55x-0.60x NAV or about 4.5x forward earnings.

Bull target: R15.00 / $0.90, up 221% from $0.28. This assumes Bankenveld visibility, debt reduction from non-core sales, continued memorial parks growth, and a re-rating to roughly 0.75x forward NAV or 6x FY28 earnings.
